findings.title: statSync blocks event loop on segment route
findings.description: statSync(sourcePath) is called synchronously after the async resolveFilePath() returns. On a network-mounted music library (e.g. NFS, SMB, or a slow spinning disk) this blocks the Node.js event loop for tens to hundreds of milliseconds per segment request. Under concurrent HLS playback (multiple users, or a single user skipping rapidly) this can cause visible latency on all other in-flight requests. serveFile() in trackFileResolver.ts also calls statSync() for the same reason (line 145), making it a double block for segment requests that need transcoding.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace statSync(sourcePath) with 'const stat = await stat(sourcePath)' using fs/promises.stat. For serveFile(), similarly switch to fs/promises.stat internally, or accept the stat object as a parameter so callers can pass the already-fetched async stat.

findings.title: TOCTOU: statSync on sourcePath can throw unhandled error if file deleted after readability check
findings.description: resolveFilePath() calls access(localPath, R_OK) to verify readability. Then the segment route calls statSync(sourcePath). If Lidarr re-downloads the file (replaces it) or an admin removes it between these two calls, statSync will throw an uncaught ENOENT. The error is NOT caught in the try/catch that wraps the segment handler (statSync is inside the handler's try block, so it will be caught by the next(err) at the bottom), but it will produce a generic 500 instead of a meaningful 404, and the stack trace leaks in development. Additionally, if the file is gone and an in-flight ensureSegment() is running for that path, its ffmpeg command will also fail, which is handled gracefully. The main risk is a confusing 500 rather than 404.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap statSync in a try/catch that maps ENOENT to createError(404, 'NOT_FOUND', 'Track file no longer available'). Alternatively use fs/promises.stat with proper error mapping.

findings.title: lidarrRootPromise not cleared on failure — permanently poisons root-folder cache
findings.description: getLidarrRootPath() stores the in-flight promise in the module-level lidarrRootPromise variable. If Lidarr is temporarily unavailable when the first stream request arrives, getRootFolders() rejects, and the rejected promise stays in lidarrRootPromise forever. Every subsequent call to getLidarrRootPath() returns the same already-rejected promise, meaning no stream request can ever succeed until the process restarts — even after Lidarr comes back online. lidarrRootPath is only set on success, so the fast path (lidarrRootPath !== null) is never reached. The bug exists because there is no .catch() or .finally() on the promise to clear lidarrRootPromise on rejection.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a .catch cleanup: lidarrRootPromise = (async () => { ... })().catch(err => { lidarrRootPromise = null; throw err; }); This lets the next caller retry the Lidarr API call instead of replaying the stale rejection.

findings.title: parseBitrate(undefined) = 320 causes bitrate mismatch between playlist and segment
findings.description: parseBitrate returns 320 when the bitrate query parameter is absent (raw === undefined). The playlist embeds bitrate=128 in every segment URI when the client requests 128 kbps. The segment route receives bitrate=128 from the query param. However, if any client or proxy ever strips query parameters from the segment URL, parseBitrate would default to 320, causing the segment to be generated at 320 kbps. The computeTrackKey hashes the bitrate into the cache key, so a 128-kbps playlist segment URL and a 320-kbps default would produce different keys and different files, resulting in double transcoding and silent audio quality inconsistency. The risk is low for well-behaved clients but higher for HLS proxies or CDN edge nodes that may strip unknown query params.
findings.suggested_fix: Change parseBitrate to return null (forcing a 400 error) when the bitrate param is absent from a segment request, and reserve the 320 default only for the playlist endpoint. Alternatively document the default explicitly with a comment. A stricter option: make bitrate required on both endpoints.

findings.title: Empty token embedded in playlist — playlist fetchable without valid auth but segments fail with 401
findings.description: If ?token= is absent or not a string, the playlist handler sets token = '' and embeds token= (empty) in every segment URI. The playlist itself is returned successfully (the authenticate middleware already passed for the playlist request). A client that then tries to fetch a segment with the embedded empty token will get 401. This means the playlist can be returned without valid embedded tokens, but playback silently fails at the first segment. This is not a security vulnerability (segments still require auth), but it is a correctness bug for clients using token-in-URL authentication. The ?token= query param is the primary auth mechanism for libmpv/media_kit on Windows, so this would manifest as silent 401s on every segment.
findings.suggested_fix: If the token is empty after extraction, either return a 400 error from the playlist handler ('token query parameter required'), or pass the already-validated JWT from the authenticate middleware context to buildPlaylist rather than re-reading it from the query string.

findings.title: Flutter seekTo: stale _segmentOffset causes unnecessary playlist reload on rapid seeks
findings.description: _segmentOffset is updated asynchronously from the mpv 'path' property observer (observeProperty fires when mpv loads each new segment URL). In seekTo(), currentSegment is computed from _segmentOffset.inMilliseconds ~/ segmentMs. If the observer fires late (mpv hasn't yet reported the new segment URL after the previous _reopenQueueAt call completes), _segmentOffset will still reflect the old segment, and targetSegment == currentSegment may be falsely true or false. A false positive (they match when they shouldn't) causes seekTo() to call _p.seek(withinSegment) within the wrong segment, producing a seek error. A false negative (they differ when they shouldn't) triggers an unnecessary _reopenQueueAt(). The false-negative case is low-risk (just redundant work); the false-positive case produces a silent seek to the wrong position.
findings.suggested_fix: Track the 'pending' segment offset as an instance variable set synchronously in seekTo() before _reopenQueueAt() is called (it already does this on line 484: _segmentOffset = Duration(milliseconds: targetSegment * segmentMs)). The same-segment fast path on line 478 correctly reads _segmentOffset before any await, so _segmentOffset reflects the last completed or pending seek, not the last observer-fired value. This is actually correct behavior on the happy path. The risk is only if seekTo() is called concurrently from two callers — Dart is single-threaded so this is impossible unless there is a re-entrant call chain via the observer callback, which does not currently exist. Confidence adjusted: this is more advisory than a confirmed bug.

findings.title: buildPlaylist last-segment EXTINF can be negative for duration rounding errors
findings.description: The last-segment duration is computed as: durationSec - i * segmentSeconds (where i = segmentCount - 1 and segmentCount = Math.ceil(durationMs / (segmentSeconds * 1000))). If Lidarr returns durationMs that is an integer-rounded estimate (common for most audio containers), the computed last-segment duration could be slightly negative when the actual audio content is slightly shorter than the estimated durationMs. For example: durationMs = 24001 ms, segmentSeconds = 6, segmentCount = ceil(24001/6000) = 5, lastSegDuration = 24.001 - 4*6 = 0.001 s. That is fine. But if durationMs = 23999 ms, segmentCount = ceil(23999/6000) = 4, lastSegDuration = 23.999 - 3*6 = 5.999 s. Also fine. However if ffmpeg's actual audio is shorter than durationMs, mpv will receive an EXTINF that overstates the actual segment duration, which is only an advisory value for HLS players and generally tolerated. The inverse (negative EXTINF) is theoretically impossible given Math.ceil, but floating-point arithmetic with non-integer segmentSeconds could produce it. HLS_SEGMENT_SECONDS is coerced to a number via Zod, so non-integer values are allowed (e.g. HLS_SEGMENT_SECONDS=5.5). If segmentSeconds is 5.5 and durationMs is 11001: segmentCount=ceil(11001/5500)=ceil(2.000181...)=3, lastSegDuration=11.001 - 2*5.5 = 0.001 s (fine). But with HLS_SEGMENT_SECONDS=5.5, TARGETDURATION would be '5.5' which violates the HLS spec (must be an integer). This is the real risk.
findings.suggested_fix: Add Math.max(0, ...) to the last-segment duration calculation. Additionally, enforce that HLS_SEGMENT_SECONDS is a positive integer in the Zod schema (z.coerce.number().int().positive()) and ensure TARGETDURATION is Math.ceil(segmentSeconds) in buildPlaylist.

findings.title: ensureSegment: generateSegment failure leaves inFlightSegments entry deleted but segment missing — next caller re-transcodes correctly
findings.description: When generateSegment rejects, the .finally() handler deletes the key from inFlightSegments. A new caller that arrives after the delete will call generateSegment again (retry). This is the correct behavior. However, all callers that shared the original promise will receive the same rejection. If an HTTP response has not yet been sent to those callers, the error propagates to next(err) and the client gets a 500. This is correct. No correctness bug exists here, but it is worth noting that under thundering-herd conditions (many concurrent requests for the same segment, ffmpeg fails), all concurrent requesters get 500 simultaneously, which may cause the client to retry and re-trigger the failing transcode in a loop. This is an advisory note, not a bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Advisory only. Consider adding a per-segment failure backoff or a maximum retry counter to avoid thundering-herd retranscode loops when the source file is consistently unreadable.

findings.title: LidarrTrack.duration confirmed to be milliseconds — segmentCount calculation is correct
findings.description: Concern raised in the review prompt: whether track.duration from Lidarr is in milliseconds. Confirmed correct: Lidarr's /api/v1/track endpoint returns duration in milliseconds. The test fixtures (trackFileResolver.test.ts line 128, hls.test.ts TRACK_DURATION_MS=240000, library.test.ts duration=240000) consistently use millisecond values and the math is correct: Math.ceil(240000 / (6 * 1000)) = 40 segments. The shapeTrack() function in libraryService.ts passes t.duration directly, and track_tile.dart calls _formatDuration(milliseconds) treating it as milliseconds (divides by 1000 to get seconds). The convention is consistent end-to-end. No action needed.
findings.suggested_fix: No fix required. Add a doc comment on LidarrTrack.duration in lidarr.ts: '// Duration in milliseconds (Lidarr API returns ms for tracks)' to prevent future confusion.

findings.title: Segment index 000 returned for segment route when segmentIndex is an out-of-bounds three-digit string like '040' for a 40-segment track
findings.description: The route param :index is parsed with parseInt(req.params['index'] as string, 10). For a 40-segment track (indices 000-039), segment/040 would have segmentIndex=40, segmentCount=40, and segmentIndex >= segmentCount is true (40 >= 40), returning 400. This is correct. However, the playlist embeds three-digit zero-padded indices (segment/039), and if the track has more than 999 segments (unlikely but possible for a 6000+ second / 100+ minute track), the padStart(3) still pads only to 3 digits. A 1001-segment track would produce segment/1000 in the playlist, which parseInt parses correctly to 1000. This is only a cosmetic inconsistency (padding does not affect parsing) and does not cause incorrect behavior. Advisory only.
findings.suggested_fix: Advisory: pad to 4 digits if segmentCount > 999, or simply remove zero-padding from segment index entirely since it serves no functional purpose for URL routing.

findings.title: HLS_SEGMENT_SECONDS env var accepts non-integer floats — violates HLS TARGETDURATION spec
findings.description: The Zod schema uses z.coerce.number().default(6) for HLS_SEGMENT_SECONDS, which allows fractional values like 5.5. RFC 8216 (HLS spec) Section 4.4.3.1 requires #EXT-X-TARGETDURATION to be a decimal-integer (rounded up). buildPlaylist uses segmentSeconds directly in the TARGETDURATION line without rounding. A non-integer value would produce an invalid playlist that some strict HLS parsers (Apple's AVPlayer on iOS) may reject. The EXTINF values also use segmentSeconds.toFixed(3) which is fine for non-last segments, but the TARGETDURATION violation is a spec issue.
findings.suggested_fix: Change Zod schema to z.coerce.number().int().positive().default(6) for HLS_SEGMENT_SECONDS. In buildPlaylist, use Math.ceil(segmentSeconds) for TARGETDURATION as a defense-in-depth measure.

findings.title: seekTo with index change calls jumpTo then re-reads _currentIndex — jumpTo clears _startSegments
findings.description: In _MediaKitBackend.seekTo(), when index != null && index != _currentIndex, the code first calls 'await jumpTo(index)'. jumpTo() calls _startSegments.clear() and _segmentOffset = Duration.zero before calling _reopenQueueAt(). After jumpTo() returns, seekTo() reads _currentIndex (which is now the new index), and then computes targetSegment from pos. Since _segmentOffset was reset to Duration.zero by jumpTo(), currentSegment will always be 0. If pos falls within segment 0, the seek succeeds with _p.seek(withinSegment). If pos falls outside segment 0, _startSegments[targetIndex] = targetSegment will be set and _reopenQueueAt() will be called a second time. This double-open of the playlist is wasteful but not incorrect. However, the first _reopenQueueAt() inside jumpTo() causes mpv to start loading from segment 0, and the second _reopenQueueAt() immediately follows with a startSegment override — mpv's load of the first playlist may partially complete before being interrupted by the second open, potentially causing a brief audio glitch or a race in mpv's demuxer state. This is a sequence correctness issue rather than a data corruption bug.
findings.suggested_fix: If seekTo is called with both a new index and a non-zero position, skip the jumpTo() call and go directly to computing the targetSegment from pos and calling _reopenQueueAt(targetIndex, withinSegment) once. Pseudo-code: if (index != null && index != _currentIndex) { _startSegments.clear(); _segmentOffset = Duration.zero; _currentIndex = index; _indexCtrl.add(index); } then compute targetSegment and call _reopenQueueAt once.

residual_risks: Lidarr's track.duration field is treated as authoritative for segmentCount computation but is derived from container metadata which can differ from actual audio duration by hundreds of milliseconds. This can cause the last segment to be slightly shorter or longer than expected, which is within HLS spec tolerance but may cause a brief click at the last-segment boundary on strict decoders.

The HLS cache directory cleanup prunes directories older than 24 hours by directory mtime (statSync(dir).mtimeMs). On Linux, writing a new segment file into an existing directory does NOT update the directory mtime in all filesystems (e.g. relatime-mounted ext4). This means a frequently-accessed cache directory could be pruned even while segments in it are still being served. This would cause 500 errors for in-progress playback sessions when segments are deleted mid-stream.

The inFlightSegments Map is process-local and not shared across Node.js worker threads or multiple process instances (PM2 cluster mode). If the backend is scaled horizontally, concurrent requests to different instances for the same segment will each spawn their own ffmpeg process, defeating the deduplication. Under the current single-process PM2 config this is not a risk, but it would become one if cluster mode is enabled.

mpv's path observer callback (_updateFromPath) is called from a native thread context via media_kit's interop bridge. If Dart's isolate model does not guarantee sequential delivery relative to other async operations on _segmentOffset, there is a theoretical data race on _segmentOffset between the observer callback and seekTo(). In practice Dart's event loop serializes microtasks so this is unlikely, but it is not formally proven.

The token is embedded as a plain query parameter in every segment URI in the M3U8 playlist. The M3U8 is served with Cache-Control: no-store, but if the client caches the playlist anyway (e.g. mpv's built-in disk cache), the token in the segment URIs will persist on disk. On token expiry, cached playlists will contain stale tokens that produce 401s on segment fetch, requiring a full playlist reload.
testing_gaps: No test covers the lidarrRootPromise failure-and-retry scenario (Lidarr returns 502 on first stream request, then succeeds on second). The bug in COR-03 would only be caught by such a test.
No test exercises concurrent segment requests for the same (trackKey, segmentIndex) pair to verify that the inFlightSegments dedup map prevents duplicate ffmpeg spawning.
No integration test for the empty-token edge case (playlist fetched without ?token= produces a playlist with token= empty, then segment requests fail with 401).
No test for a non-integer HLS_SEGMENT_SECONDS value to verify TARGETDURATION spec compliance.

No test for statSync throwing ENOENT on sourcePath (file deleted between resolveFilePath and statSync). The try/catch in the route handler will catch this, but there is no assertion that it maps to 404 rather than 500.
Flutter: no widget test exercises seekTo() with both a new index and a non-zero position (the double-open race in COR-12). This scenario is difficult to unit test without a real mpv instance.
No test for the cache cleanup pruning a directory that is actively being served (COR residual risk 2).

Integration test mock ffmpeg writes a 512-byte buffer of 0xAA bytes (not valid MP3 frames). This means serveFile() tests that send the segment to a real player would fail. The tests only verify HTTP status codes and headers, not that the served bytes are valid audio — acceptable for integration tests but leaves the MP3 frame validity assumption untested.
